1: וַיְהִי כִּי-זָקֵן יִצְחָק וַתִּכְהֶיןָ עֵינָיו מֵרְאֹת (wayhî kî-zāqēn yiṣḥāq wattikheynā ʿênāyw mērĕʾōt) — "when Isaac was old and his eyes grew dim from seeing." Kāhāh (Qal: to be dim, faint, darkened) — the same verb of eye-dimming used for Eli in 1 Samuel 3:2 and the Servant's description of not quenching a smoldering wick in Isaiah 42:4. Isaac's blindness is the narrative pivot of the entire chapter; it creates the conditions for the deception. The stark contrast with Deuteronomy 34:7 — Moses at 120 whose eyes had not dimmed (lōʾ-kāhătāh ʿênô) — is implicit: Moses' eyes held; Isaac's do not. The dimming is a narrative device, not a theological judgment.
2: לֹא יָדַעְתִּי יוֹם מוֹתִי (lōʾ yāḏaʿtî yôm môtî) — "I do not know the day of my death." The declaration of existential ignorance creates urgency — Isaac believes he is near death; the narrative's irony is that Isaac will live many more years and witness Jacob's departure and Esau's family formation (Genesis 26:34-35 already described Esau's marriages; Genesis 28:6-9 happens before Isaac dies, and Genesis 35:27-29 records his death at 180). Isaac's sense of imminent death is premature, which makes the urgency of the blessing scene both real and ironic.
3: כְּלֶיךָ תֶּלְיְךָ וְקַשְׁתֶּךָ (kĕlêḵā tĕlyĕḵā wĕqaštĕḵā) — "your weapons, your quiver and your bow." Tĕlî (quiver, the case for arrows — from תָּלָה, to hang; that which hangs from the shoulder) — the hunting equipment identifies Esau's domain; he is the outdoor hunter, the man of the field (Genesis 25:27). Isaac's commission to Esau is built on their established relational pattern: hunt game, cook, bring to me — this has presumably happened before; now it is to precede the deathbed blessing.
4: וְעַשֵׂה-לִי מַטְעַמִּים כַּאֲשֶׁר אָהַבְתִּי (wĕʿăśēh-lî maṭʿammîm kaʾăšer ʾāhaḇtî) — "prepare for me delicacies as I love them." Maṭʿam (delicacy, tasty food, savory dish) from ṭāʿam (taste); the word appears first in 25:28 where Isaac loved Esau "because game was in his mouth" (kî-ṣayiḏ bĕpîw). Isaac's love for Esau is anchored in sensory pleasure — taste. בַּעֲבוּר תְּבָרֶכְךָ נַפְשִׁי (so that my soul may bless you) — napšî (my soul) — Isaac speaks of his nepeš blessing Esau, not merely his mouth or his authority; the whole self, the life-breath, the volitional center, confers the blessing. The nepeš-blessing is the most solemn form of personal benediction.
5: וְרִבְקָה שֹׁמַעַת (wĕriḇqāh šōmāʿat) — "and Rebekah was listening." The Qal participle šōmāʿat (was listening/hearing) — the continuous aspect of the participle implies sustained, deliberate attention: Rebekah was positioned to hear and did hear the entire exchange. The narrative does not editorialize whether she was eavesdropping or happened to overhear — the Hebrew simply states the fact. The oracle she received in Genesis 25:23 ("the elder will serve the younger") gives her interpretive context for what she now hears; her action in Genesis 27:6-17 is driven by that prior divine word.
6: Rebekah immediately summons Jacob and transmits what she heard — exact reported speech of Isaac's commission to Esau. The precision of her quotation is notable: she repeats Isaac's words faithfully (with one addition in Genesis 27:7 — "in the presence of YHWH," which may reflect her own theological framing of the deathbed blessing). The mother's intervention is grounded in the oracle of Genesis 25:23, though she does not cite it explicitly; her action presupposes the divine word as her authorization.
7: לִפְנֵי יְהוָה לִפְנֵי מוֹתִי — "in the presence of YHWH before my death." Rebekah adds "before YHWH" to her quotation of Isaac's words — Isaac's original commission (Genesis 27:4) does not include this phrase. Whether Rebekah inserts it for rhetorical impact (to motivate Jacob) or whether she genuinely heard it in a longer exchange the narrator did not record is indeterminate. The addition elevates the blessing from a family ceremony to a covenantal religious act performed before the divine witness.
8: שְׁמַע בְּקֹלִי (šĕmaʿ bĕqōlî) — "listen to/obey my voice." The imperative šĕmaʿ + preposition bĕ (to hear in accordance with) = to obey, follow the instructions of. Rebekah assumes command authority over Jacob; the language is the same as a divine command (cf. Exodus 19:5: "if you hear/obey my voice"). The irony: Rebekah commands Jacob to obey her (šĕmaʿ bĕqōlî), and Jacob's obedience to his mother will lead to a blessing he sought by disobedience to his father's plan.
9: שְׁנֵי גְּדָיֵי עִזִּים טֹבִים (šĕnê gĕḏāyê ʿizzîm ṭōḇîm) — "two choice young goats." Gĕḏî ʿizzîm (kid of the goats) — young male goat; the specification of two goats (plural) is practical: sufficient meat to prepare a meal that will pass for fresh-hunted game. The goat is domesticated livestock (from the flock), not game (from the field) — the substitution of domestic animal for wild game is the core deception in terms of the food itself. Isaac presumably recognizes the difference in flavor, but his sense of smell (Genesis 27:27, the clothes) and touch (Genesis 27:22-23, the goatskins) are manipulated to override any taste-suspicion.
10: The plan's goal: וְאָכַל בַּעֲבוּר אֲשֶׁר יְבָרֶכְךָ לִפְנֵי מוֹתוֹ — "so that he may eat and bless you before his death." The chain: Jacob brings the food → Isaac eats → Isaac blesses. The blessing is contingent on the food; the food is the medium of the blessing encounter. The deception therefore operates through the food: replace the food (game with goat), replace the recipient's identity, receive the blessing intended for another.
11: הֵן עֵשָׂו אָחִי אִישׁ שָׂעִר וְאָנֹכִי אִישׁ חָלָק (hēn ʿēśāw ʾāḥî ʾîš śāʿir wĕʾānōḵî ʾîš ḥālāq) — "behold, my brother Esau is a hairy man and I am a smooth man." Śāʿir (hairy — from śēʿār, hair; also related to śāʿîr, goat/satyr) — the physical distinction between the twins was already stated at birth (Genesis 25:25: Esau came out "red and hairy all over"). Ḥālāq (smooth, slippery — from the same root as ḥēleq, portion/flattery; the smoothness is Jacob's literal skin quality). Jacob's objection is not moral ("I shouldn't deceive my father") but practical ("I won't fool him because our skin feels different") — the text records this without moral commentary.
12: וְהָבֵאתִי עָלַי קְלָלָה וְלֹא בְרָכָה (wĕhāḇēʾtî ʿālay qĕlālāh wĕlōʾ ḇĕrāḵāh) — "I would bring curse upon myself and not blessing." Jacob uses the pair qĕlālāh (curse) and bĕrāḵāh (blessing) — the antonyms that dominate the chapter. His fear is of the curse-mechanism of false swearing: invoking blessing through deception inverts the blessing into curse. The theological realism is precise: a blessing received through deception risks becoming a curse. Rebekah's response in Genesis 27:13 assumes the curse-risk and accepts it personally.
13: עָלַי קִלְלָתְךָ בְּנִי (ʿālay qillāṭĕḵā ḇĕnî) — "upon me be your curse, my son." Rebekah accepts the curse-risk in a formula that deliberately echoes covenant curse-assumption language. The tragic irony: Rebekah's curse-acceptance is partially fulfilled — she will never see Jacob again (Genesis 27:45 suggests she expects a "few days"; the few days become years; Genesis 27:46-28:5 sends Jacob to Paddan-Aram, from which he returns only in Genesis 31, after Rebekah has died — she is not mentioned as alive at his return). The "curse" she accepts is separation from her beloved son for the rest of her life.
14: Jacob obeys: he goes, gets the goats, brings them to Rebekah. The series of waw-consecutive imperfects is fast: wayyēleḵ wayyiqqaḥ wayyāḇēʾ (he went, he took, he brought). No hesitation, no further moral deliberation. Jacob moves from objection to obedience without transition — once Rebekah accepts the curse, he acts. Wattaʿaś ʾimmô maṭʿammîm kaʾăšer ʾāhab ʾāḇîw (his mother prepared delicacies as his father loved them) — the same phrase from Genesis 27:4 (what Isaac loves) is fulfilled by Rebekah's cooking, not Esau's hunting.
15: בִּגְדֵי עֵשָׂו בְּנָהּ הַגָּדֹל הַחֲמֻדֹת (biḡḏê ʿēśāw bĕnāh haggāḏōl haḥămuḏōt) — "the finest clothes of Esau her older son." Ḥămuḏōt (choice, desirable, precious — plural of excellence from ḥāmaḏ, to desire, covet — the same root as the tenth commandment's prohibition on coveting, Exodus 20:17). Esau's "desirable garments" carry the smell of the field (Genesis 27:27), which is what triggers Isaac's blessing. The clothes are kept in Rebekah's custody (ʾittāh babbāyit, with her in the house) — either because Esau stored good clothes at home or because Rebekah had possession of them.
16: וְאֵת עֹרֹת גְּדָיֵי הָעִזִּים הִלְבִּישָׁה עַל-יָדָיו — "she covered his hands and smooth neck with goatskins." The tactile deception: the goatskins (ʿōrōt gĕḏāyê hāʿizzîm) replicate Esau's hairiness on Jacob's smooth hands and neck. The skin substitution is direct: Jacob, who does not have Esau's śēʿār (hair), is given goat-ʿôr (skin) to simulate it. The Hebrew wordplay between śēʿîr (Seir, the land of Edom/Esau) and śāʿir (hairy) is not explicit here but underlies the whole narrative — Esau's identity is bound up in his hairiness and his land.
17: Rebekah hands Jacob the prepared food and the bread. The provision is complete: the disguise (clothes + goatskin), the food (delicacies + bread). Jacob goes to his father with everything needed to complete the deception. The narrative is spare and sequential — no interior monologue, no hesitation from Jacob at the threshold. The story is driven by events and dialogue, not by psychological exposition.
18: וַיָּבֹא אֶל-אָבִיו וַיֹּאמֶר אָבִי — "he came to his father and said 'my father.'" וַיֹּאמֶר הִנֶּנִּי מִי אַתָּה בְּנִי — "he answered 'here I am — who are you, my son?'" Isaac's immediate question mî ʾattāh bĕnî (who are you, my son?) is the question the entire chapter hinges on. The question will be asked again in Genesis 27:24 (hăʾattāh zeh bĕnî ʿēśāw). Isaac cannot see and must rely on other senses — which have been systematically manipulated. The hinnēnî (here I am) of Isaac's response echoes the readiness-formula that marks covenant encounters (Genesis 22); here it is Isaac who is present and available, but to be deceived.
19: אָנֹכִי עֵשָׂו בְּכֹרֶךָ (ʾānōḵî ʿēśāw bĕḵōreḵā) — "I am Esau your firstborn." The lie is direct, emphatic (ʾānōḵî, the strong first-person pronoun), and complete: identity + birth-order. Jacob claims the two things he most lacks: Esau's name and Esau's firstborn status. עָשִׂיתִי כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבַּרְתָּ אֵלָי (I have done as you said to me) — the second lie: Isaac told Esau to hunt game; Jacob was sent by Rebekah to get goats. He claims his mother's commission as his father's. קוּם-נָא שְׁבָה וְאָכְלָה מִצֵּידִי (rise now, sit, and eat of my game) — calling domestic goat "my game" (ṣêḏî, from ṣûḏ, to hunt) is the third lie. Three deceptions in one verse.
20: מַה-זֶּה מִהַרְתָּ לִמְצֹא בְּנִי — "how did you find it so quickly, my son?" Isaac's practical suspicion: Esau's hunting would take hours; Jacob has returned too fast for game-hunting. כִּי הִקְרָה יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ לְפָנַי (kî hiqrāh YHWH ʾĕlōhêḵā lĕpānay) — "because the LORD your God caused it to happen before me." Hiqrāh is the Hiphil of qārāh (to meet, happen, encounter) — the identical verb the servant used in 24:12 when he prayed for genuine divine providential arrangement. Jacob attributes to YHWH a false providential success: the very vocabulary of honest prayer in Genesis 24 becomes the vocabulary of lying in Genesis 27. Jacob invokes YHWH's name in his deception — the theological weight is precise and disturbing.
21: גְּשָׁה-נָּא וַאֲמֻשְׁךָ בְּנִי (gĕšāh-nnāʾ waʾămušĕḵā bĕnî) — "come near so I may feel/touch you, my son." Māšaš (to feel, grope, touch for verification) — the diagnostic touch; Isaac substitutes his remaining functional senses (touch, smell, Genesis 27:27; hearing, Genesis 27:22) for his lost sight. הַאַתָּה זֶה בְּנִי עֵשָׂו אִם-לֹא (whether you really are my son Esau or not) — Isaac's direct skepticism is expressed; the syntax is a bipartite question with ʾim-lōʾ (or not): he is not fully persuaded and needs sensory confirmation. The touch-test is about to fail him.
22: הַקֹּל קוֹל יַעֲקֹב וְהַיָּדַיִם יְדֵי עֵשָׂו (haqqōl qôl yaʿăqōḇ wĕhayyāḏayim yĕḏê ʿēśāw) — "the voice is the voice of Jacob, but the hands are the hands of Esau." This is one of the most structurally elegant lines in Genesis — a perfect chiasm built on the perceptual conflict: voice (hearing) identifies Jacob; hands (touch) falsely identify Esau. The chiastic structure (voice-Jacob // hands-Esau) mirrors the conflict between two senses giving contradictory evidence. Rabbinical tradition reads this line as the paradigmatic statement of Jewish history: Jacob's voice (Torah) may dominate, but when Jacob's hands become like Esau's (violence), Esau prevails.
23: וְלֹא הִכִּירוֹ כִּי-הָיוּ יָדָיו כִּידֵי עֵשָׂו אָחִיו שְׂעִרֹת — "he did not recognize him because his hands were hairy like the hands of his brother Esau." Hikkîr (Hiphil of nākar, to recognize, identify, distinguish) — the failure of recognition is stated flatly. Isaac cannot nākar (recognize) his own son. The irony: the root nākar will be used when Joseph's brothers fail to recognize him in Egypt (Genesis 42:8: "Joseph recognized his brothers, but they did not recognize him"). Failure to recognize by sight, by touch — recognition is the chapter's negative subplot. Wayyĕḇārăḵēhû (and he blessed him) — the blessing is given in genuine but deceived intention.
24: הַאַתָּה זֶה בְּנִי עֵשָׂו (haʾattāh zeh bĕnî ʿēśāw) — "are you really my son Esau?" A second direct question — Isaac's doubt persists even after touching; he must ask again. ʾĂnî (I am) — Jacob's response is the shortest possible affirmation; one word, unembellished, without the rhetorical elaboration of Genesis 27:19. The simple "I am" (ʾănî) in answer to "are you Esau?" is technically true only if we read it as "I am [here, present, the one speaking to you]" — but the question admits only one reading in context. Jacob answers the question he was not asked.
25: Isaac eats and drinks — the ritual meal that forms the context for the covenantal blessing. Wayyāšet lô yayin wayyēšt (he brought him wine and he drank) — wine as part of the solemn meal; the blessing that follows is given in an elevated, formal state. The eating and drinking before blessing mirrors the covenant ratification meals of the OT (Exodus 24:11: the elders "ate and drank" before YHWH at Sinai; the Last Supper covenant meal context).
26: גְּשָׁה-נָּא וּשְׁקָה-לִּי בְּנִי (gĕšāh-nnāʾ ûšĕqāh-llî bĕnî) — "come near and kiss me, my son." The kiss (nāšaq) is both an act of filial affection and the physical proximity that allows Isaac to smell the clothing — the final sense-check before the blessing. The sensory progression: touch (Genesis 27:21-23), hearing (Genesis 27:22), taste (Genesis 27:25), now smell (Genesis 27:27). Every sense is engaged in the blessing encounter; the deception has been engineered to deceive all four.
27: וַיָּרַח אֶת-רֵיחַ בְּגָדָיו (wayyārāḥ ʾet-rêaḥ bĕḡāḏāyw) — "he smelled the smell of his garments." Rûaḥ / rêaḥ (the smell/scent; from the same root as rûaḥ, spirit/breath — both are about essence perceived through the breath). Isaac's blessing begins with a sensory declaration: רֵאֵה רֵיחַ בְּנִי כְּרֵיחַ שָׂדֶה אֲשֶׁר בֵּרֲכוֹ יְהוָה (the smell of my son is like the smell of a field that YHWH has blessed) — the field (śādeh) is Esau's domain (Genesis 25:27: "Esau was a man of the field"); Isaac associates the outdoor smell with Esau's identity and with YHWH's blessing on the created order. The blessing opens with a creation metaphor: the fertile, YHWH-blessed field.
28: וְיִתֶּן-לְךָ הָאֱלֹהִים מִטַּל הַשָּׁמַיִם וּמִשְׁמַנֵּי הָאָרֶץ — "may God give you the dew of heaven and the fatness of the earth." The divine name shifts: the blessing uses hāʾĔlōhîm (the God) rather than YHWH — the more universal divine title, appropriate for a general agricultural blessing. Ṭal (dew) is the life-giving moisture of Canaan's dry summer months; mišmannê (fatness/richness — Hiphil noun from šāman, to be fat, prosperous) = the productive richness of fertile soil. Wĕrōḇ dāḡān wĕtîrōš (abundance of grain and new wine) — the two agricultural staples of the settled land; this blessing is the blessing of possession and agricultural productivity.
29: יַעַבְדוּךָ עַמִּים וְיִשְׁתַּחֲווּ לְךָ לְאֻמִּים — "may peoples serve you and nations bow down to you." The blessing transitions from agricultural to political: from produce to dominion. הֱוֵה גְבִיר לְאַחֶיךָ (be lord/master over your brothers) — geḇîr (master, lord — male form of gĕḇirāh, queen-mother; the masculine form is rare and formal, used only here and in Genesis 27:37 and in Isaiah 26:13); the lordship of Jacob over Esau is the fulfillment of Genesis 25:23 ("the elder shall serve the younger"). אֹרְרֶיךָ אָרוּר וּמְבָרְכֶיךָ בָּרוּךְ — "cursed are those who curse you and blessed are those who bless you." The covenant formula of Genesis 12:3 (Abraham's call: "those who curse you I will curse, those who bless you I will bless") is now transmitted through Isaac to Jacob — the covenant blessing-and-curse mechanism is formally transferred to the next generation.
30: אַךְ יָצֹא יָצָא יַעֲקֹב (ʾaḵ yāṣōʾ yāṣāʾ yaʿăqōḇ) — "as soon as Jacob had gone out." The emphatic infinitive absolute yāṣōʾ yāṣāʾ (had just barely gone out, had scarcely departed) — the timing is dramatically precise: Jacob exits and Esau immediately enters; the near-miss is as close as possible. wĕʾēśāw ʾāḥîw bāʾ miṣṣêḏô (his brother Esau came in from his hunting) — the word ṣêḏ (hunting, game) — Esau brings genuine game from genuine hunting; the contrast with Jacob's domestic-goat substitute is implicit but present.
31: יָקֻם אָבִי וְיֹאכַל מִצֵּיד בְּנוֹ — "let my father rise and eat of his son's game." Esau uses the same invitation formula as Jacob in Genesis 27:19 — "rise and eat" (qûm ʾĕḵol) — and the same word ṣêḏ (game) — but now it is genuine: genuine son, genuine game. The parallelism between Esau's speech and Jacob's earlier impersonation is a narrative device: when the real version appears, the deception that preceded it becomes more visible by comparison.
32: מִי-אַתָּה (mî-ʾattāh) — "who are you?" Isaac asks Esau the same question he asked Jacob in Genesis 27:18 (mî ʾattāh bĕnî). Esau answers truthfully: "I am your son, your firstborn, Esau." The word bĕḵōrĕḵā ʿēśāw (your firstborn Esau) repeats exactly the false claim Jacob made in Genesis 27:19 — heard now after the deception is complete, it registers as the truth that was displaced. Isaac must confront: the person who said these words before was lying; the person saying them now is telling the truth, and the blessing is gone.
33: וַיֶּחֱרַד יִצְחָק חֲרָדָה גְּדֹלָה עַד-מְאֹד (wayyeḥĕraḏ yiṣḥāq ḥărāḏāh gĕḏōlāh ʿaḏ-mĕʾōḏ) — "and Isaac trembled a very great trembling." The emphatic infinitive absolute construction (ḥăraḏ ḥărāḏāh gĕḏōlāh ʿaḏ-mĕʾōḏ) — the strongest Hebrew grammar for intensity: he trembled a trembling great exceedingly. Ḥāraḏ (to tremble, shake — typically in the context of divine encounter, fear of judgment, or extreme alarm; cf. Exodus 19:18 when Sinai trembled at YHWH's presence; 1 Samuel 4:13 when the city trembled at the battle news). וְגַם-בָּרוּךְ יִהְיֶה (and moreover he shall be blessed) — Isaac's trembling is not only shock but theological recognition: the blessing given under deception is irrevocable; the word has gone out and cannot be recalled. The blessing has its own ontological weight — it is not merely Isaac's wish but a covenantal pronouncement that stands.
34: צְעָקָה גְּדֹלָה וּמָרָה עַד-מְאֹד (ṣĕʿāqāh gĕḏōlāh ûmārāh ʿaḏ-mĕʾōḏ) — "a great and very bitter cry." Ṣāʿaq (to cry out — often in contexts of distress seeking divine hearing: Exodus 2:23; Genesis 14:10; Genesis 18:20-21 for Sodom's cry) — Esau's cry is at the same decibel level as a community crying to God for relief. Mārāh (bitter) — the bitterness of the cry evokes Mārāh, the bitter water of Exodus 15:23; the bitter wailing of Rachel for her children (Jeremiah 31:15); bitterness as the taste of loss and injustice. Hebrews 12:17 will cite this moment: "he found no place of repentance, though he sought it with tears."
35: בָּא אָחִיךָ בְּמִרְמָה וַיִּקַּח בִּרְכָתֶךָ (bāʾ ʾāḥîḵā bĕmirmāh wayyiqqaḥ birḵāteḵā) — "your brother came with deceit and took your blessing." Mirmāh (deceit, treachery — from rāmāh, to deceive, beguile; used also for Jacob's deception in Hosea 12:3 and the broader judgment oracle). Isaac names the act without ambiguity: mirmāh is moral language — deceit as a deliberate act of betrayal. Yet Isaac does not rescind the blessing; the word stands. The declarative sentence has a resigned quality: this is what happened, and it cannot be undone.
36: הֲכִי קָרָא שְׁמוֹ יַעֲקֹב וַיַּעְקְבֵנִי זֶה פַּעֲמַיִם (hăḵî qārāʾ šĕmô yaʿăqōḇ wayyaʿqĕḇēnî zeh paʿămāyim) — "is it not rightly that his name is Jacob? For he has supplanted me (yaʿqĕḇēnî) these two times." Esau's wordplay: yaʿqōḇ (Jacob, from ʿāqēḇ, heel — the grasping-heel motif of 25:26 when Jacob grabbed Esau's heel at birth) → yaʿqĕḇēnî (he has supplanted/deceived me — the denominative verb from the name, meaning "he has Jacob-ed me"). The accusation encodes the name's dual meaning: Jacob = heel-grabber = one who supplants by deceptive maneuvering. Hosea 12:3 will use the same wordplay: "in the womb he grasped the heel (ʿāqaḇ) of his brother." ʾEt-bĕḵōrātî lāqaḥ (he took my birthright) — the birthright was sold (Genesis 25:33) but Esau's "he took" rewrites the transaction as theft; selective memory is part of the brother's cry.
37: הֵן גְּבִיר שַׂמְתִּיו לָךְ (hēn gĕḇîr śamtîw lāḵ) — "behold, I have made him lord/master over you." Isaac states the blessing's content in summary: lordship (geḇîr), possession of all relatives, grain and wine (dāḡān wĕtîrōš) — everything of substance has been given to Jacob. וּלְךָ אֵפוֹ מָה אֶעֱשֶׂה בְּנִי (so what can I do for you, my son?) — ʾēpôʾ (then, so, what now) — the particle of resigned inquiry; Isaac acknowledges the emptiness of his position. There is no equivalent blessing remaining; the entire content of the deathbed blessing has been given away under deception.
38: הֲבְרָכָה אַחַת הִיא-לְךָ אָבִי (hăḇĕrāḵāh ʾaḥat hîʾ-lĕḵā ʾāḇî) — "have you only one blessing, my father?" Esau's question presses the theological premise: is the deathbed blessing a finite resource? The answer is yes in this case — the main blessing has been given. Esau's cry (wayiśśāʾ ʿēśāw qōlô wayyēḇk, he lifted up his voice and wept) uses the mourning formula; the weeping is the same expression as Hagar's and Ishmael's at the expulsion (Genesis 21:16) and Joseph's when he reveals himself (Genesis 45:2). The tears of loss in the patriarchal narratives are the narrative's emotional register for irrevocable events.
39: הִנֵּה מִשְׁמַנֵּי הָאָרֶץ יִהְיֶה מוֹשָׁבֶךָ — "behold, from the fatness of the earth will be your dwelling." The Esau oracle (Genesis 27:39-40) is syntactically ambiguous: the preposition min before mišmannê (fatness) can mean "of" (sharing in the fatness) or "away from" (separated from the fatness). The LXX and most modern translations prefer "away from" — the dwelling is away from productive farmland, in the rocky terrain of Edom/Seir. Ûmiṭṭal haššāmayim mēʿāl (and away from the dew of heaven above) — the negation of Jacob's blessing: where Jacob receives the agricultural bounty, Esau receives its absence. The oracle is not a curse but a realistic geographic forecast for Edomite settlement territory.
40: וְעַל-חַרְבְּךָ תִחְיֶה (wĕʿal-ḥarbĕḵā tiḥyeh) — "by your sword you will live." Esau's oracle shifts from agriculture (absent) to warfare (present): the sword (ḥereḇ) is Esau's means of subsistence where Jacob has grain and wine. וְהָיָה כַּאֲשֶׁר תָּרִיד (wĕhāyāh kaʾăšer tārîḏ) — "but when you grow restless" — tārîḏ (Hiphil of rûḏ, to rove, wander, have unrestricted movement; in the Hiphil: to achieve dominance, break free) — when Edom becomes strong enough, the yoke will be thrown off. The oracle is both realistic (Edom will be subdued by David, 2 Samuel 8:14) and conditional-reversible (Edomite revolts in 2 Kings 8:20-22 partially fulfill the "throw off the yoke" clause).
41: וַיִּשְׂטֹם עֵשָׂו אֶת-יַעֲקֹב (wayyiśṭōm ʿēśāw ʾet-yaʿăqōḇ) — "and Esau bore a grudge against Jacob." Śāṭam (to hate with hostility, bear a grudge, persecute — a stronger word than ordinary śānēʾ, hate; it implies sustained, active, hostile resentment; cf. 49:23 and 50:15 where the same root describes the hostility Joseph's brothers feared). יִמְרְאוּ יְמֵי אֵבֶל אָבִי (the days of mourning for my father are near) — Esau's plan is timed: after Isaac's death (the mourning days), he will kill Jacob. The plan shows both family loyalty (waiting for the mourning period) and murderous intent — the combination recalls Cain (Genesis 4:5-8) in its internal logic.
42: וַיֻּגַּד לְרִבְקָה אֶת-דִּבְרֵי עֵשָׂו בְּנָהּ הַגָּדֹל — "Rebekah was told the words of her older son Esau." The passive wayyuggad (it was reported/told) — someone informed Rebekah; the narrative does not say who; the information network of the household is assumed. תְּנַחֵם לְךָ לְהָרְגֶךָ (tĕnaḥem lĕḵā lĕhorgĕḵā) — "he is consoling himself by planning to kill you." Nāḥam (to comfort, console, regret) in the reflexive: Esau is comforting himself (lĕḵā, reflexive dative) by planning revenge. The irony: nāḥam (comfort) and murder planning are combined — Esau's grief-processing is murderous. The same verb will be used positively when Isaac is comforted by Rebekah in Genesis 24:67.
43: בְּרַח-לְךָ אֶל-לָבָן אָחִי חָרָנָה (bĕraḥ-lĕḵā ʾel-lāḇān ʾāḥî ḥārānāh) — "flee to Laban my brother in Haran." Bāraḥ (to flee, run away) — the imperative of urgent escape; the same verb used for the flight of Moses from Egypt (Exodus 2:15) and the flight of David from Absalom. Jacob must flee because Esau is planning murder. Rebekah directs Jacob to her own brother Laban in Haran — the same place from which she came (Genesis 24:10); Jacob's flight will put him in the household of his mother's family, fulfilling the narrative thread begun with the servant's mission in Genesis 24.
44: יָמִים אֲחָדִים (yāmîm ʾăḥāḏîm, a few days) — Rebekah anticipates that the separation will be brief; the same phrase is used in 29:20 where Jacob's seven years of labor for Rachel "seemed like only a few days" because of his love. The irony: Rebekah's "few days" of Jacob's absence becomes approximately twenty years. She never sees him again after his departure. The words "few days" are the narrative's saddest understatement in the patriarchal section.
45: לָמָה אֶשְׁכַּל גַּם-שְׁנֵיכֶם יוֹם אֶחָד (lāmmāh ʾeškkal gam-šĕnêḵem yôm ʾeḥāḏ) — "why should I be bereaved of both of you in one day?" Šāḵal (to be bereaved of children — the word for child-loss, used for Rachel weeping for her children in Jeremiah 31:15; for the bereaving of Egypt in Exodus 12:29; for the Servant bearing the grief of the bereaved in Isaiah 53:10). Rebekah fears a double bereavement: if Esau kills Jacob, the blood-guilt means Esau faces execution ("blood for blood") — she would lose both sons in one day. Wayyĕšallĕḥēhû — the send-verb that runs through the patriarchal narratives: Rebekah now sends Jacob just as Abraham's servant was sent (Genesis 24:7).
46: קַצְתִּי בְחַיַּי מִפְּנֵי בְּנוֹת חֵת (qaṣtî ḇĕḥayyay mipĕnê ḇĕnôt ḥēt) — "I am disgusted with my life because of the Hittite women." Qûṣ (to be disgusted, loathe — used for Israel's disgust with manna in Numbers 21:5; for Rebekah's own disgust with Esau's marriages). Rebekah's complaint to Isaac about the Hittite wives (cf. Genesis 26:34-35: Esau's marriages to Judith and Basemath, Hittite women, which "made life bitter for Isaac and Rebekah") is the pretext she uses to send Jacob away without revealing the real reason (Esau's murder threat). The ruse works: Isaac is persuaded to formally send Jacob to Paddan-Aram for a wife, which gives Jacob's flight the appearance of a legitimate mission rather than an escape.
